turns.content: what's the difference between workspace dependencies and shared proto definitions in a monorepo?

The core distinction lies in how you manage versioning across your crates. Workspace dependencies, like cargo:dev or cargo:build, are handled by the root Cargo.toml and apply to every crate automatically without needing explicit overrides. Shared proto definitions, on the other hand, require a dedicated internal crate that exposes types via traits like impl Serialize for use in multiple packages. The difference is that workspace dependencies affect build artifacts directly, while shared protos define the contract layer that your organization's crates must adhere to.

I see, but I'm confused about how to handle versioning if everyone uses these proto types. If we update the internal crate, does it break every consumer? That feels like a common mistake beginners make when setting up their monorepo structure.

Exactly, I've seen teams fail because they treat shared protos like regular workspace dependencies without proper isolation. It's frustrating to have one breaking change cascade through the entire codebase at once.

Can you explain why this happens so I can avoid it? I need a way to understand how Rust traits and crates interact here without just following a checklist.
I don't get it. It feels like the whole organization is stuck on one version. How do we prevent that?

Think of your monorepo as a city where different buildings (crates) must speak the same language to communicate. If you just paste the dictionary definition into every building's code, changing one word breaks everything instantly because there's no separation between the source and the usage layer. The mistake is treating shared proto definitions like workspace dependencies by updating them directly in the root configuration. Instead, imagine a central library where the internal crate holds the latest edition of the dictionary. When you update that library, it doesn't force every building to rebuild immediately; they only pick up changes when they explicitly request a new version from the registry. This way, your organization can manage transitions gradually without a single point of failure affecting everyone at once.

I'm getting a compilation error when trying to use the proto definition crate in my workspace. The message says 'the trait bound for MyProtoType: Serialize cannot be satisfied' and I can't figure out which derive macro is missing or if I need to update the internal versioning of this crate.

That error usually means your custom type isn't properly implementing the Serialize trait required by serde_json, likely because you forgot to add the derive macro in the source file. Think of it like a key that doesn't fit the lock; even if your data structure is correct, without the Serialize implementation (the key), the serialization system (serde) can't access it. Before we dive deeper into how to fix the trait bound, could you tell me which specific type definition or crate version you are currently referencing in your workspace?

The issue is specifically with a shared proto package I'm trying to import from the internal crates directory. It looks like I need to update the Cargo.toml dependencies in the workspace root, but I'm not sure how to handle the publishing strategy for these shared definitions without breaking other consumers.

You're dealing with a classic monorepo challenge where shared proto definitions need consistent versioning across all crates. The solution involves updating your workspace's Cargo.toml to reference the correct internal crate path and ensuring that any newly published versions update all dependent crates simultaneously using a coordinated publishing strategy. This prevents situations where one consumer has an older version of MyProtoType than another, which would cause exactly this kind of trait bound mismatch when they try to serialize data together.

I'm trying to set up a monorepo for our team's Rust libraries, and I was thinking we should just put everything under one Cargo.toml so all crates can share the same versioning without needing workspace dependencies.

That won't work because a single Cargo.toml cannot define multiple package versions at once; you need to use the workspace feature in Cargo.toml to declare a common directory and then list each crate individually under [package] so they can all inherit dependencies like serde_json or derive macros from the root. If you don't set up the workspace correctly, your crates won't be able to reference shared proto definitions or coordinate their internal versioning properly.

I've read the documentation on workspace dependencies and shared proto definitions, but I still don't get why we can't just publish every crate individually. It feels like a gotcha that keeps tripping me up.

The confusion usually stems from thinking each binary in the workspace is a separate library meant for external distribution. In a monorepo, crates are organized under a workspace file where you define shared proto definitions and internal crate versioning rules. The key insight is that while individual crates can be published to crates.io, the monorepo structure itself keeps them tightly coupled internally before release.

Oh, I assumed that every crate in the workspace had to be versioned independently like a normal library. So if I bump a shared proto definition, does it force all dependent crates to update their versions automatically?

That's exactly the wrong assumption you made there. You don't need every crate to have its own version number for internal dependencies; instead, you use workspace-level versioning to manage shared proto definitions and ensure consistency across the organization. When you publish a new release of your monorepo, you typically bump the root version in Cargo.toml, which triggers the publishing strategy to coordinate all crates that depend on those shared definitions.
